input:
# Document

A traditional assumption, built into the structure of the U.S. criminal justice system, is that individuals who are committing crimes are physically within a geographical jurisdiction, their victims are physically present within that jurisdiction, and any necessary evidence that exists could also be obtained within that jurisdiction.

## References

1. Caldwell, Leslie R., 'Assistant Attorney General Leslie R. Caldwell Delivers Remarks at the Securities Enforcement Forum West Conference,' U.S. Department of Justice, May 12, 2016.

input:
# Document

There is concern that the necessary grid loads to power future AI data centers for training and inference activities may exceed the capacity of additions to the U.S. power grid.[[18]](#footnote-18) In this report, we assess potential increases in supply resources in the contiguous United States and propose a capacity metric that can be directly compared with load increases.

## Footnotes

[18]. Bistline et al., 2023; North American Electric Reliability Corporation, 2024; Pilz, Mahmood, and Heim, 2025.
[19]. Srivathsan et al., 2024; Scharre, 2024.
[20]. Secretary of Energy Advisory Board, 2024.
[22]. Pilz, Mahmood, and Heim, 2025.

## References

1. Bistline, John, Geoffrey Blanford, Maxwell Brown, Dallas Burtraw, Maya Domeshek, Jamil Farbes, Allen Fawcett, Anne Hamilton, Jesse Jenkins, Ryan Jones, et al., 'Emissions and Energy Impacts of the Inflation Reduction Act,' Science, Vol. 380, No. 6652, June 30, 2023.
2. Davenport, Carly, Brian Singer, Neil Mehta, Brian Lee, and John Mackay, 'Generational Growth: AI, Data Centers and the Coming US Power Demand Surge,' Goldman Sachs Equity Research, April 28, 2024.
3. North American Electric Reliability Corporation, *2024 Long-Term Reliability Assessment*, December 2024.
4. Pilz, Konstantin F., Yusuf Mahmood, and Lennart Heim, *AI's Power Requirements Under Exponential Growth: Extrapolating AI Data Center Power Demand and Assessing Its Potential Impact on U.S. Competitiveness*, RAND Corporation, RR-A3572-1, 2025. As of June 3, 2025: https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA3572-1.html
5. Scharre, Paul, *Future-Proofing Frontier AI Regulation: Projecting Future Compute for Frontier AI Models*, Center for a New American Security, March 2024.
6. Secretary of Energy Advisory Board, 'Recommendations on Powering Artificial Intelligence and Data Center Infrastructure,' U.S. Department of Energy, July 30, 2024.
7. Sevilla, Jaime, Tamay Besiroglu, Ben Cottier, Josh You, Edu Roldán, Pablo Villalobos, and Ege Erdil, 'Can AI Scaling Continue Through 2030?' Epoch AI, August 20, 2024.
8. Shehabi, Arman, Sarah J. Smith, Alex Hubbard, Alex Newkirk, Nuoa Lei, Md Abu Bakar Siddik, Billie Holecek, Jonathan Koomey, Eric Masanet, and Dale Sartor, *2024 United States Data Center Energy Usage Report*, Berkeley Lab, December 2024.
9. Srivathsan, Bhargs, Marc Sorel, Pankaj Sachdeva, Arjita Bhan, Haripreet Batra, Raman Sharma, Rishi Gupta, and Surbhi Choudhary, 'AI Power: Expanding Data Center Capacity to Meet Growing Demand,' McKinsey & Company, October 2024.
name: citation_power_capacity_footnote_18_multiple_refs

# Document

The anticipated load growth from AI is extraordinary in magnitude, speed, and geographic concentration. Developing advanced AI models requires enormous amounts of power,[[19]](#footnote-19) and existing facilities are already straining the U.S. electricity grids.[[20]](#footnote-20)

## Footnotes

[18]. Bistline et al., 2023; North American Electric Reliability Corporation, 2024; Pilz, Mahmood, and Heim, 2025.

input:
# Document

Estimates for data center demand growth in the United States by 2030 vary widely, from an additional 34 gigawatts (GW) to as much as 253 GW. This variability stems from significant uncertainties, including the pace of the growth in demand, potential efficiency improvements through innovation, and the unclear long-term expansion plans of data center operators. Differences in reporting units (e.g., terawatt-hours [TWh] versus GW), scope (AI-specific versus general data center demand), and projection timelines further contribute to the range of estimates:

## Footnotes

[18]. Bistline et al., 2023; North American Electric Reliability Corporation, 2024; Pilz, Mahmood, and Heim, 2025.
[19]. Srivathsan et al., 2024; Scharre, 2024.
[20]. Secretary of Energy Advisory Board, 2024.
[22]. Pilz, Mahmood, and Heim, 2025.

As of June 3, 2025: https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA3572-1.html

# Document

- RAND projects that global AI data center power demand could reach 327 GW by 2030.[[22]](#footnote-22) For the United States alone, additional demand is estimated to be between 158 GW and 253 GW, assuming a retention rate of 50 percent to 80 percent.

# Document

With fleet-wide mission capable rates at a historic low of 62% in FY2024—leaving nearly 1,900 aircraft unavailable on any given day—and a persistent shortage of approximately 1,850 pilots, the service confronts systemic challenges across pilot production and retention, aircraft availability, and critical infrastructure.[[1](#fn1)] [[2]](#fn2)

## References

1. [https://www.defensenews.com/air/2025/03/06/air-force-aircraft-readiness-plunges-to-new-low-alarming-chief/](https://www.defensenews.com/air/2025/03/06/air-force-aircraft-readiness-plunges-to-new-low-alarming-chief/)

input:
# Document

This assessment identifies key vulnerabilities across three operational pillars and proposes evidence-based, non-partisan policy recommendations to restore combat effectiveness. The analysis reveals that decades of deferred investment, compounded by an aging fleet averaging 32 years old and a $49.5 billion infrastructure maintenance backlog, have created cascading readiness shortfalls that require immediate attention. With China rapidly expanding its military capabilities and the Air Force operating at the smallest force structure in its 78-year history (5,025 aircraft), addressing these systemic gaps is imperative to maintaining deterrence and warfighting capacity.[[3](#fn3)] [[4](#fn4)] [[1]](#fn1)

## References

1. [https://www.defensenews.com/air/2025/03/06/air-force-aircraft-readiness-plunges-to-new-low-alarming-chief/](https://www.defensenews.com/air/2025/03/06/air-force-aircraft-readiness-plunges-to-new-low-alarming-chief/)

# Document

Using this framework, we assessed 22 sites: 17 identified by DOE, two private-sector developments by the Stargate consortium, and three recently retired or retiring power plants.[[10]](#footnote-10)[[11]](#footnote-11)

## Footnotes

[8]. In Task 1, we developed a detailed inventory of all ISO generation projects currently in the pipeline as well as historical data on completion rates by resource type and ISO.
[9]. We shared earlier versions of our framework with three organizations which provide energy services to datacenters across the United States.
[10]. These three sites were selected from the U.S. Energy Information Administration's EIA-860 Monthly Generator Data as large generators either retired within the past five years or scheduled for retirement before 2030. The selection process and criteria are described in Appendix C, "Maximum Capacity at an Individual Site."
[11]. Office of Policy, Department of Energy, "Request for Information on Artificial Intelligence Infrastructure on DOE Lands."
[12]. Arciniegas Rueda et al., "Barriers and Solutions for Expanding U.S Net Available Power Capacity by 2030"
[13]. Pilz, "Trends in AI Supercomputers."
[14]. Our focus was on the sites contained in the U.S. Department of Energy's March 2025 Request for Information on Artificial Intelligence Infrastructure on DOE Lands.

## References

1. Arciniegas Rueda, Ismael, et al., Additional AI Power Capacity by 2030, RAND Corporation, 2025.
2. Secretary of Energy Advisory Board, Recommendations on Powering AI Infrastructure, U.S. Department of Energy, July 2024.
3. North American Electric Reliability Corporation, 2024 Long-Term Reliability Assessment, December 2024.
